timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Feeding of the Five Thousand

sections.header: Verses 1–23 — What Defiles: Tradition vs Command of God

heb.paragraph: Corban — a vow dedicating property to the Temple. The Pharisees allowed a son to declare his assets Corban, effectively making them unavailable to help aging parents while still retaining personal use. Jesus exposes this as a mechanism for legally violating the fifth commandment while appearing pious.

refs.note: These people honour me with their lips but their hearts are far from me — the text Jesus quotes directly. Isaiah’s charge against eighth-century Israel becomes Jesus’s charge against first-century Pharisaism.
Honour your father and your mother — the command the Corban tradition subverts.
I will give you a new heart — the prophetic promise of internal transformation that Jesus’s teaching about internal defilement anticipates.

mar.source: Joel Marcus, Mark 1–8 / 8–16, Anchor Bible (2000/2009) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Marcus: The parenthetical explanation of Jewish purity customs for non-Jewish readers is among the strongest internal evidence for Mark's Gentile-Roman audience. Marcus notes that Mark is the only Gospel that explains these customs, suggesting a community not observant of or familiar with Second Temple Jewish practice. The Mishnah (tractate Yadayim) provides extensive discussion of hand-washing before meals, confirming this was a genuine Pharisaic practice, not a Markan caricature.

Marcus: The Corban practice is attested in Josephus (Against Apion 1.167) and in a Qumran fragment (4Q270). Marcus argues that the rabbinic sources (Mishnah Nedarim) show the sages were themselves uncomfortable with vows that disadvantaged parents — suggesting Jesus's critique was an internal Jewish debate, not an attack on Torah observance per se. The force of the criticism is not that the tradition is Jewish but that it has been used to nullify the higher principle it claimed to honour.

rho.source: David Rhoads & Donald Michie, Mark as Story (3rd ed., 2012) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Rhoads: The clean/unclean controversy has a three-part structure: dispute (7:1-13), public teaching (7:14-16), private explanation (7:17-23). This structure mirrors the parable sections of ch.4: public pronouncement that confuses, followed by private clarification for insiders. The narrator's interjection in 7:19 ("In saying this, Jesus declared all foods clean") is the narrator speaking directly to the reader, interpreting Jesus's teaching for a community apparently living under the implications of that teaching.

Rhoads: "Jesus left that place and went to the vicinity of Tyre. He entered a house and did not want anyone to know it; yet he could not keep his presence secret." The tension between Jesus's desire for hiddenness and his unavoidable notoriety is a recurring narrative pattern in Mark. Rhoads identifies this as a characterization technique: the Messianic Secret operates as an element of plot tension, not just theology. Every time Jesus withdraws, he is found; every time he silences testimony, it spreads. The character cannot be contained.

Chrysostom notes that the Pharisees' challenge about unwashed hands is not about hygiene but about the authority of oral tradition. Jesus's response cuts to the root: the tradition has been used to nullify the command. The Corban example is, for Aquinas, an instance of legal ingenuity deployed against moral obligation — a person could declare property dedicated to God and thereby escape the duty to support parents. Bede observes that Jesus quotes Isaiah 29:13 precisely: "their hearts are far from me" — the critique is not of ritual practice as such but of ritual practice divorced from inward reality.

The teaching on defilement — "Nothing outside a person can defile them" — is, for Origen, the most radical dietary pronouncement in the Synoptics: Mark notes parenthetically that Jesus "declared all foods clean" (v19). Chrysostom says this is not the abolition of the law but its fulfilment: the law's food codes were pedagogical, pointing toward the interior purity that alone makes a person acceptable to God. The catalogue of vices in vv.21-22 is, for Theophylact, a comprehensive anatomy of the sinful heart — beginning with evil thoughts and ending with foolishness, showing that moral failure begins in the mind.

You have let go of the commands of God and are holding on to human traditions — the reversal is the charge: what was meant to protect the Torah has replaced the Torah. Tradition has swallowed the text it claimed to honour.

Jesus declared all foods clean — Mark’s editorial comment, not part of Jesus’s direct speech. This parenthetical interpretation by the narrator is the most significant food-law statement in the Synoptics and becomes the theological basis for Peter’s Cornelius vision (Acts 10).

You have a fine way of setting aside the commands of God — Calvin reads this as the defining critique of all religion that substitutes human invention for divine command. The Reformation’s critique of tradition-over-Scripture is exegetically grounded here.

All foods clean — Calvin notes that this anticipates the abolition of the ceremonial law in Christ. The dietary laws were shadows of the covenant reality. Their fulfilment in Christ releases their binding force.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Corban (korbanas) — a loanword from Hebrew qorban (offering). The practice of corban vows is attested in Mishnah Nedarim 9:1, which shows that rabbis were themselves uncomfortable with vows that disadvantaged parents. Jesus’s criticism reflects a genuine abuse within the tradition.

“In saying this” — the phrase is a participial clause describing Jesus’s action: as he said this, he was pronouncing all foods clean. Mark’s editorial recognition of the implication is confirmed in Acts 10:9-16 (Peter’s vision) and Acts 15:28-29 (Jerusalem council decision).

hist.context: The clean/unclean controversy is the theological centre of Mark’s Galilean section. Jesus makes two claims: (1) external ritual cannot defile the heart — the oral tradition overrides the written Torah; (2) only internal evil defiles — overturning the Levitical purity system entirely. Mark’s editorial note (v.19: “In saying this, Jesus declared all foods clean”) is one of the most significant theological parentheses in the NT.

heb.paragraph: Ephphatha is the second Aramaic word preserved verbatim in Mark (after Talitha koum in 5:41). The opening of ears and tongue in a Gentile region fulfils Isaiah 35:5-6 — the eschatological opening of the deaf and the mute is happening, and it is happening in Gentile territory.

refs.note: Then will the ears of the deaf be unstopped…and the mute tongue shout for joy — the text fulfilled in v.35 is the eschatological healing of the new creation.
Matthew’s parallel explicitly commends the woman’s great faith — Mark omits this but it is implicit in Jesus’s response.
My house will be called a house of prayer for all nations — the inclusion of Gentiles in the covenant that the Syrophoenician episode inaugurates.

mar.source: Joel Marcus, Mark 1–8 / 8–16, Anchor Bible (2000/2009) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Marcus: The withdrawal to Tyre and Sidon is the deepest penetration of Gentile territory in Mark's narrative. Tyre was notorious in OT tradition as the exemplar of pagan wealth and pride (Ezek 26-28; Isa 23; Joel 3:4-8). That Jesus goes there and heals a Gentile woman in her homeland represents the fulfilment of the OT promises that even Israel's most notorious enemies would eventually receive divine grace (Isa 23:17-18; Zech 9:3-4).

Marcus: "Ephphatha" (be opened) — the Aramaic preserved in the healing command echoes Isa 35:5 ("the ears of the deaf will be unstopped"). Crucially, Isa 35 is addressed to Zion in the context of the new exodus: the deaf will hear as part of the eschatological restoration. That this healing happens in Gentile territory (the Decapolis) is Mark's implicit claim that the eschatological healing of Israel has been extended to include the nations — fulfilling the prophetic horizon that Isa 35 itself anticipated (cf. Isa 35:1-2, the wilderness flourishing).

rho.source: David Rhoads & Donald Michie, Mark as Story (3rd ed., 2012) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Rhoads: The Syrophoenician woman is characterized through dialogue alone — the most dialogue-intensive exchange with a minor character in Mark. Her speech is economical, theologically precise, and rhetorically skilled. She accepts the premise of Jesus's statement (children first) and finds the logical room within it (crumbs remain for dogs). Rhoads argues this is the narrative's clearest example of a character who "out-argues" Jesus — and in doing so, demonstrates the faith that the disciples consistently fail to show.

Rhoads: The circuitous route from Tyre through Sidon to the Sea of Galilee via the Decapolis is geographically odd — Sidon is north of Tyre, Decapolis is south of Galilee — but narratively intentional. Mark keeps Jesus in Gentile territory for an extended period before returning to Jewish space. The route signals that the Gentile mission is not a brief excursion but a sustained engagement. Rhoads's narrative geography argument: the map of Mark's narrative is the map of the kingdom's expansion.

The Syrophoenician woman's encounter is, for Chrysostom, the most theologically concentrated dialogue in Mark. Jesus's initial refusal ("It is not right to take the children's bread and toss it to the dogs") is not, for Origen and Ambrose, a statement of prejudice but a test of faith and a teachable moment about the order of redemptive history — Israel first. The woman's response accepts the metaphor and turns it: dogs under the table eat the children's crumbs. This, says Chrysostom, is the highest form of faith — to hold on to hope even when the apparent word is discouragement.

The healing of the deaf-mute involves a series of physical gestures — fingers in the ears, touching the tongue, spitting, looking up to heaven — which Bede says are not magical acts but signs of condescension: Jesus works through material means to meet a person whose faith is in its infancy. The Aramaic command "Ephphatha" (Be opened) is preserved by Mark with special weight — Origen notes that the same word applies to the ears of spiritual hearing. The crowd's reaction "He has done everything well" is, for Chrysostom, an unwitting echo of Genesis 1: the Creator's work is good.

It is not right to take the children’s bread and toss it to the dogs — the remark appears harsh but the diminutive “dogs” (kynarion — household dogs, not street curs) is less harsh than it sounds. The priority of Israel is real (Rom 1:16: first to the Jew); the woman’s reply accepts the priority and claims the overflow.
He looked up to heaven with a deep sigh — the sigh (stenaxas) is the same root as the groaning of creation in Rom 8:22-23. Jesus groans in solidarity with the disorder he is about to reverse.

Calvin: The answer about dogs and children is a test rather than a refusal. Jesus is eliciting the faith he knows is there. The woman’s reply shows that she understands the covenant order (children first) and trusts in the covenant’s generosity (crumbs are enough). Her theology is sounder than many of Israel’s insiders.

Ephphatha — Calvin: the Aramaic command is preserved to show that the healing is by authority, not formula. The word effects what it says. The deaf hear not because of the ritual (fingers, spittle, sigh) but because the word of the Lord is irresistible.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: A Greek, born in Syrian Phoenicia — the double designation is precise. She is ethnically Greek (Hellēnis: culturally Hellenistic) and racially Syrophoenician. She represents the Gentile world at its most culturally sophisticated — not a barbarian but a Hellenized woman of the coastal trading cities.

Deep sigh (stenaxas) — the verb indicates emotional intensity. Jesus’s healings in Mark are not clinical but emotionally engaged. He is not a dispassionate miracle machine but a person who shares in the weight of the condition he is removing.

hist.context: The movement into Tyre and Sidon is the deepest penetration of Gentile territory in Mark’s Gospel. The Syrophoenician woman’s exchange with Jesus is among the most theologically charged conversations in the NT: Jesus’s “dogs” remark and her brilliant riposte turn on the question of the covenant’s scope. Her persistence wins a Gentile healing and a commendation of her faith. The deaf man’s healing in the Decapolis then fulfils Isaiah 35’s eschatological vision.

ppl.name: The Syrophoenician woman
ppl.text: Her brilliant two-sentence dialogue with Jesus is among the most theologically charged in the Gospels. She accepts the covenant priority order and claims the overflow. Jesus grants her request “for such a reply.”

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"First let the children eat all they want... for it is not right to take the children's bread and toss it to the dogs." "Lord," she replied, "even the dogs under the table eat the children's crumbs."ESV"Let the children be fed first, for it is not right to take the children's bread and throw it to the dogs." But she answered him, "Yes, Lord; yet even the dogs under the table eat the children's crumbs."KJV"Let the children first be filled: for it is not meet to take the children's bread, and to cast it unto the dogs." And she answered and said unto him, "Yes, Lord: yet the dogs under the table eat of the children's crumbs."NASB"Let the children be satisfied first, for it is not good to take the children's bread and throw it to the dogs." But she answered and said to Him, "Yes, Lord, but even the dogs under the table feed on the children's crumbs."NLT"First I should feed the children — my own family, the Jews. It isn't right to take food from the children and throw it to the dogs." She replied, "That's true, Lord, but even the dogs under the table are allowed to eat the scraps from the children's plates."

src.title: Mishnah Nedarim 9:1
src.quote: Rabbinic text showing that the sages themselves were uncomfortable with corban vows that disadvantaged parents.
src.note: Confirms that Jesus’s critique of the Corban practice was a genuine legal debate within Judaism, not an outsider’s misunderstanding.

rec.text: Augustine used the Syrophoenician woman’s exchange to argue that the covenant’s overflow to Gentiles was always its inner logic — the priority of Israel was temporal and pedagogical, not exclusionary.

rec.who: Martin Luther, Commentary on the Gospels
rec.text: Luther used the Syrophoenician woman as his primary example of what he called “struggling faith” — faith that persists against apparent refusal and holds God to his own promise.

hebtext.note: Corban — a vow dedicating property to the Temple. The Pharisees allowed a son to declare his assets Corban, effectively making them unavailable to help aging parents while still retaining personal use. Jesus exposes this as a mechanism for legally violating the fifth commandment while appearing pious.

Ephphatha is the second Aramaic word preserved verbatim in Mark (after Talitha koum in 5:41). The opening of ears and tongue in a Gentile region fulfils Isaiah 35:5-6 — the eschatological opening of the deaf and the mute is happening, and it is happening in Gentile territory.

Corban — a vow dedicating property to the Temple. The Pharisees allowed a son to declare his assets Corban, effectively making them unavailable to help aging parents while still retaining personal use. Jesus exposes this as a mechanism for legally violating the fifth commandment while appearing pious.

tx.title: "With a fist" or "carefully/up to the elbow"
tx.content: NA28readspugmē(with a fist — a specific washing gesture); some interpret as "carefully" or "frequently." The term is unclear and unique in the NT.
tx.note: The hapax legomenon pugmē is almost certainly original (no scribe would insert an obscure term); the meaning is debated but the ritual washing context is secure.

tx.title: "Let anyone with ears to hear, listen"
tx.content: Sinaiticus, Vaticanusomit 7:16 entirely; it appears in later MSS as a scribal addition harmonising with 4:9.
tx.note: The verse is almost certainly secondary — a scribe imported 4:9's refrain. The NIV brackets it or omits it. Its absence does not affect the argument of 7:14-15.

positions.name: Test-of-faith view
positions.proponents: Jesus knew the woman’s faith and was drawing it out. The diminutive kynarion (household dogs) is less insulting than the street-dog slur.

positions.name: Priority-of-Israel view
positions.proponents: Jesus genuinely maintained the covenant priority (Israel first) and only extended healing after the woman’s persistent faith demonstrated Gentile receptivity.

themes.note: Mark 7 is the purity and mission chapter: the clean/unclean controversy dissolves the barrier between Jewish and Gentile space, enabling the Syrophoenician and Decapolis healings.

coaching.tip: 'Nothing outside a person can defile them' (v. 15). Jesus overturns purity categories: defilement isn't external contamination but internal corruption. Mark adds: 'In saying this, Jesus declared all foods clean' (v. 19).

The Syrophoenician woman accepts the 'dog' metaphor and turns it: 'Even the dogs under the table eat the children's crumbs' (v. 28). Her persistence and wit win the day. Gentile faith shames Jewish rejection.
